entry.title: SAP just launched the Business Suite - what does this mean for Finance?
entry.content:
Feb 13, 2025 is “Business Unleashed Day” for the SAP Community, with announcements on SAP Business Suite and SAP Business Data Cloud - and how AI, data, and applications are combined like never before to help you unleash your full potential and make you unstoppable.

Whether your company has been running an SAP ERP solution for many years, or whether you’re just at the beginning of the evaluation of a Cloud ERP solution, you might be wondering what impact these announcements – especially the announcements of the SAP Business Suite - will have on your landscape planning.

Evolution from monolithic to composable

Just as technology has completely changed since ERP was first defined for 1970s mainframes, SAP’s approach to ERP has evolved to keep up you keep us with the needs of your business:

  • Modular capabilities that can be adopted quickly as new needs arise
  • Always up-to-date software-as-a-service solutions that give you non-disruptive access to innovations
  • Clear interoperability and data semantics so that you will be in the best position to realize the benefits of AI

The 2025 Gartner® ERP Primer for 2025 states that “in Gartner’s 2023 ERP, Procurement, HCM and Finance Apps Survey of those manager level and above who are responsible for ERP decision making, 71% reported their organizations were either in process or have plans to adopt a flexible and agile ERP architecture within two years.”

So, chances are, you’re already thinking about the importance of having the flexibility to choose business technology more flexibly to support your growth initiatives.

 

 

Gartner: ERP Primer for 2025, by Denis Torii, Dixie John. GARTNER is a registered trademark and service mark of Gartner, Inc. and/or its affiliates in the U.S. and internationally and is used herein with permission. All rights reserved.

 

The SAP Business Suite is SAP’s answer to Composable ERP

You know SAP as a leader in Cloud ERP for years, but the “ERP and Finance” focus of finance teams may overlook the additional, incremental value SAP can provide with our broader family of solutions – solutions that address business needs and can be adopted incrementally, like Fieldglass, Ariba, SuccessFactors, Concur, and CX.

Since the interoperability and data semantics among the applications is curated by SAP, the insights that can be achieved with SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Business AI are limitless.

 

RISE with SAP is a transformation journey aimed at helping SAP ERP customers transition to the SAP Business Suite - so they can stay ahead of the competition by continuously innovating – across their whole enterprise landscape – not just in “ERP”.

What this means is that Finance teams should be thinking of the value that SAP solutions can create in your IT landscape. For example, adopting updated, world-class procurement processes have benefits specifically for the CFO in terms of cash visibility, risk mitigation and more. You can read a great blog on the topic here.

The SAP Business Suite approach encourages companies to think beyond their administrative ERP footprint: how can we adopt the best solutions to solve all types of business problems – for the Chief Revenue Officer, or for the Chief Human Resources Officer?

Cloud software and software-as-a-service promised that business teams would be able to pick-and-choose the services that meet their business requirements. However, often IT does have to be involved to manage integrations and ensure data interoperability. The SAP Business Suite aims to make that modularity - and business user choice - promise a reality.  

And modularity is not just part of the North Star architecture at the line of business level. SAP Business Suite is a reminder that within the line of business solutions, there are software-as-a-service solutions that can be adopted to solve specific business problems – even if your current core architecture might be more monolithic than you’d like.

Composability within Financial Management

Even within the areas that are traditionally considered to be “ERP”, SAP is offering more flexibility and modularity than ever before. Based on your business needs, these SaaS solutions – with their out-of-the-box best practices and constant access to innovation - are available as extensions to SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

  • SAP Green Ledger - to align your carbon and financial accounting by integrating carbon emissions with your business processes, complementing SAP’s portfolio of sustainability solutions, including SAP Sustainability Control Tower.
  • SAP Advanced Financial Closing – for orchestrating financial closing activities across ERP systems
  • SAP Account Substantiation and Automation by BlackLine – to automate manual closing tasks and reconciliations
  • SAP Digital Banking – to automate connections to any financial service provider (banks, SWIFT, payment service providers) in a quick and easy way for more control
  • SAP Cloud for Credit Integration – to integrate and manage external credit rating information for completely touchless, responsive credit decisionmaking
  • SAP Trading Platform Integration – to connect to leading trading platform providers for inbound and outbound straight-through-processing of trades 
  • SAP Risk and Assurance Management - to automate compliance checks and manage remediations efficiently

These solutions – that solve specific business problems- are integrated with our standard financial management processes in Cloud ERP.

So, if you’re part of the 70% of ERP leaders thinking about flexibility in ERP, you should consider what the SAP Business Suite can do for your organization. Making the most of the SAP Business Suite will help you

  • Manage uncertainty through effective steering and agile planning
  • Navigate transformation supported by efficient processes to balance growth and profitability
  • Enable compliance and sustainability in an ever-evolving regulatory and risk environment

Learn more about Finance solutions here.

entry.title: Why the CPO and the Office of the CPO Should Care About Finance
entry.content:
The role of the Chief Procurement Officer (CPO) has undergone a significant transformation in recent years. Traditionally viewed as an operational function focused on price negotiation and tactical sourcing, procurement is now recognized as a strategic pillar integral to the overall financial health and strategic direction of the organization.

The central argument of this document is that as the CPO's responsibilities expand to encompass value creation, risk management, and alignment with corporate financial goals, a robust understanding of finance becomes paramount. This necessitates a shift from a transactional mindset to a strategic partnership with the finance department.

In the competitive landscape of today’s business world, it is essential that CPOs grasp the critical importance of understanding finance. By integrating concepts like agency theory[1], resource dependence theory[2], and transaction cost economics[3], CPOs can align procurement goals with company finances, manage supplier relationships effectively, and streamline procurement processes. This integration delivers tangible benefits across key areas of the company and reinforces the core message that a collaborative relationship between procurement and finance is essential for achieving sustainable competitive advantage.

Enabling Cost Management and Efficiency

A core responsibility of the CPO is to drive cost management and efficiency improvements. To achieve this effectively, a deep understanding of financial principles is essential.

Understanding Financial Constraints

The CPO must possess a thorough understanding of the company's financial constraints, including budget cycles, capital allocation processes, and working capital management. This knowledge enables the CPO to align procurement strategies with available resources and financial targets.

For example, in the manufacturing industry, aligning procurement with budget cycles and implementing just-in-time inventory management can reduce inventory holding costs by up to 15%.

Contributing to Budget Accuracy

The CPO's office plays a vital role in providing accurate data for budgeting and forecasting. Precise forecasting of procurement costs, including direct materials, indirect expenses, and services, is critical for creating realistic and reliable budgets.

By leveraging historical data, market intelligence, and predictive analytics, the CPO can improve budget accuracy by up to 20% in industries such as healthcare, where precise cost control is paramount.

Justifying Procurement Investments

CPOs must be able to articulate the financial benefits of procurement investments using recognized financial metrics such as Return on Investment (ROI), Net Present Value (NPV), and payback period. A well-constructed business case, supported by rigorous financial analysis, is essential for securing funding for strategic initiatives such as procurement technology implementations or supplier development programs.

In the technology sector, demonstrating an ROI of 150% or higher for procurement technology investments is a common benchmark for success. This enables a well-documented method that stakeholders can consider to make a proper decision and can be backed by numbers.

Supporting Risk Mitigation

Risk mitigation is an increasingly important aspect of the CPO's role, and financial considerations are central to effective risk management.

Financial Risk Assessment of Suppliers

CPOs must conduct thorough financial risk assessments of key suppliers to ensure their financial stability and ability to consistently deliver goods and services. This includes analysing suppliers' financial statements, credit ratings, and market positions.

In the automotive industry, proactive assessment of supplier financial risk has been shown to reduce supply chain disruptions by as much as 30%.

Ensuring Compliance with Financial Regulations

Procurement activities must adhere to all relevant financial regulations and compliance standards to avoid fines, penalties, and legal liabilities. This includes ensuring compliance with anti-corruption laws, such as the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) in the United States, tax regulations like the General Anti-Avoidance Rule (GAAR) in Canada, and international accounting standards such as IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards).

In the pharmaceutical industry, rigorous compliance with financial regulations can significantly reduce legal and reputational risks, potentially by up to 25%.

Currency Risk Management

For organizations with global supply chains, currency fluctuations can have a significant impact on procurement costs. CPOs must work closely with finance to develop and implement strategies for managing currency risk, such as utilizing financial instruments or negotiating contracts in local currencies. Financial instruments are tools used in the financial markets for borrowing, lending, and investing.

Examples include options, futures, forwards, swaps, and other derivatives that can help hedge against fluctuations in currency exchange rates. Effective currency risk management can save organizations approximately 10% in costs, particularly in sectors like retail with extensive international sourcing.

Driving Value Creation

Beyond cost savings and risk mitigation, procurement can play a proactive role in driving value creation and innovation.

Demonstrating Procurement's Financial Impact

CPOs must effectively communicate the financial impact of procurement initiatives to key stakeholders, including senior management and the board of directors. This requires tracking and reporting key performance indicators (KPIs) such as cost savings, revenue growth, and return on assets.

In the energy sector, strategic sourcing initiatives and supplier relationship management programs have been shown to increase profitability by 5% or more. This not only highlights the strategic importance of procurement but also reinforces the core message that a strong procurement-finance partnership is vital for organizational success.

Collaborating on Innovation Funding

When procurement identifies opportunities for innovation, the CPO must collaborate closely with finance to secure funding and resources. This includes developing compelling business cases, presenting financial projections, and aligning innovation initiatives with the company's strategic priorities.

In the consumer goods industry, collaborative partnerships between procurement and finance have been shown to increase market share by as much as 12%.

Supporting Sustainable and Ethical Sourcing

Sustainable and ethical sourcing practices can enhance a company's brand reputation, increase customer loyalty, and drive sales growth. CPOs should work with finance to develop and implement sustainable sourcing strategies that align with the company's environmental, social, and governance (ESG) goals. For instance, sourcing materials from suppliers who adhere to environmentally friendly practices or fair labor standards can improve the company’s image.

In the fashion industry, sustainable sourcing has been shown to improve brand reputation by 40%, leading to a 10% increase in sales. This practice not only contributes to long-term value creation but also emphasizes the core message of aligning procurement with broader corporate financial and ethical goals.

Leveraging Financial Data for Decision Making

Data-driven decision-making is essential for effective procurement, and financial data is a critical input into this process.

Spend Analysis & Beyond

CPOs must leverage financial data to conduct comprehensive spend analysis, identify cost-saving opportunities, and reduce waste. This includes analysing spending patterns, identifying maverick spending, and negotiating better prices with suppliers.

In the telecommunications industry, effective spend analysis has been shown to cut procurement costs by 15%.

Integrating with FP&A

Procurement data should be seamlessly integrated with Financial Planning & Analysis (FP&A) processes to ensure alignment with the company's overall financial goals. This includes sharing data on procurement costs, supplier performance, and market trends.

In the aerospace sector, this integration has improved financial performance alignment by as much as 20%. This seamless integration highlights the importance of a strong procurement-finance partnership.

Technology and Digitalization

The use of technology in procurement and finance integration is crucial for enhancing efficiency, transparency, and data-driven decision-making. ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) systems streamline and centralize data from various departments, ensuring that procurement and finance are aligned in real-time. E-Procurement platforms automate the purchasing process, reducing manual errors and providing a clear audit trail for financial analysis. Data analytics tools, such as spend analysis software, help organizations identify spending patterns, monitor supplier performance, and uncover cost-saving opportunities. By leveraging these technologies, companies can optimize procurement processes, improve financial planning, and ultimately drive better business outcomes.

Conclusion

Integrating procurement and finance is essential for a competitive edge. The CFO should grasp the strategic value of procurement, while the CPO must appreciate finance's role. A strong bond with finance allows the CPO to enhance procurement's financial impact, reduce risks, generate value, and boost overall financial success. Regular cross-functional training, shared KPIs, and clear communication are recommended to promote collaboration and understanding. In summary, the close collaboration between procurement and finance departments is not just beneficial but imperative for achieving sustainable competitive advantage. CPOs and finance professionals are called to action to embrace this partnership and align their strategies for the greater financial health of their organizations.

 

[1] Relevant example: Describes potential conflicts when procurement prioritizes the lowest-priced suppliers without assessing their financial stability, potentially disrupting supply chains and impacting finance's budget accuracy.

[2] Relevant example: Dependence on a single supplier for critical components exposes the company to price volatility and supply disruptions, impacting finance's forecasting and working capital management.

[3] Relevant example: Automating invoice processing and reconciliation between procurement and finance reduces delays, errors, and administrative costs, lowering overall transaction costs

entry.title: Chart of Accounts Optimization: The First Step in Your Cloud-Ready Finance Transformation
entry.content:
Chart of Accounts Optimization: The First Step in Your Cloud-Ready Finance Transformation

Introduction

As businesses embark on their digital transformation journeys, finance leaders often find themselves at a crossroads—how to ensure their financial structures are ready for the cloud. The answer lies in optimizing the Chart of Accounts (CoA) as the first and most crucial step. An inefficient CoA can slow down reporting, complicate compliance, and hinder real-time decision-making.

On the other hand, a well-structured CoA serves as the backbone of financial transparency, automation, and scalability—making it a key enabler of cloud transformation. With SAP S/4HANA Cloud becoming the standard for enterprise finance, businesses must rethink their legacy CoA structures to ensure they are lean, scalable, and intelligent.

In this blog, we’ll explore the need for CoA optimization, introduce the SMART framework, and outline a three-step advisory roadmap to get started.

Why CoA Optimization is the First Step in Moving to the Cloud : Many organizations overlook the importance of CoA in cloud transformation. However, migrating to a cloud-based ERP with an outdated or bloated CoA can lead to inefficiencies rather than improvements.

An unstructured CoA can lead to:

❌Redundant GL accounts, making reporting complex

❌Manual reconciliations, slowing financial closing

❌Non-compliant structures, increasing audit risks

❌Inconsistent CoA across business units, causing mismatches

CoA is the Fundamental for Finance

Here’s why optimizing CoA is a non-negotiable first step:

1. Standardization for Global Operations :

A streamlined CoA eliminates redundant accounts and ensures uniform financial reporting across business units and geographies. In cloud environments, a harmonized CoA reduces complexity in multi-entity reporting.

Before Optimization: • Multiple GL accounts for similar transactions across business units (e.g., 5000 - Sales USA, 6000 - Sales Europe). • Inconsistencies in reporting structures lead to complex financial consolidation.

After Optimization: • Harmonized CoA across all entities (e.g., 5000 - Sales Revenue, with dimensions for country-wise reporting).

• Consolidated reporting becomes automated, reducing reconciliation time by 40%.

Sample CoA Structure

Result: A leaner, standardized CoA allows for real-time reporting, compliance automation, and AI-powered financial insights.

2. Real-Time Financial Insights

A well-structured CoA enables SAP S/4HANA’s Universal Journal, allowing finance teams to get real-time insights instead of batch-processed reports. This is critical for businesses leveraging predictive analytics and AI-driven decision-making.

Before Optimization: • Sales and cost data scattered across multiple accounts, requiring manual adjustments. • No direct visibility into profitability at product or region level.

After Optimization: • Optimized CoA structured for real-time tracking via SAP Universal Journal. • AI-driven insights allow predictive sales analytics, improving demand forecasting.

3. Regulatory Compliance & Audit Readiness: 

Cloud-based finance systems need to comply with multiple regulations (IFRS, GAAP, tax laws). An optimized CoA ensures compliance with built-in financial controls, reducing audit complexities.

Before Optimization: • GL accounts not aligned with IFRS/GAAP, requiring manual intervention during audits. • High risk of non-compliance due to region-specific accounting structures.

After Optimization: • CoA aligned with global and local accounting standards. • AI-based anomaly detection flags potential compliance risks in real-time.

4. Seamless Integration Across Finance & Operations  Finance is no longer an isolated function—it interacts with logistics, sales, HR, and procurement. A clean CoA structure ensures smooth data flow across all modules in SAP, enabling automation and intelligent processing.

Before Optimization: • Finance, logistics, and sales maintained separate CoA structures, causing data mismatches. • Month-end closing delayed due to manual reconciliation efforts.

After Optimization: • Unified CoA across finance and operations, allowing real-time integration with procurement and sales. • AI-powered auto-reconciliation reduces closing time by 50%.

5. AI & Automation Readiness :

 AI-powered financial automation (auto-reconciliation, predictive analytics) depends on clean, structured data. A well-optimized CoA allows machine learning models to operate efficiently, reducing manual effort in financial operations.

Before Optimization: • Legacy CoA with excessive manual processes for financial analysis. • Error-prone data hinders AI adoption for automation.

After Optimization: • AI-optimized CoA structure allows for automated journal entries and predictive financial forecasting. • AI-driven fraud detection reduces financial risks by 20%.

With these factors in mind, organizations must approach CoA optimization with a structured framework.

This is where SMART  comes into play.

Introducing the SMART  Framework for CoA Optimization The SMART framework provides a structured methodology for designing a lean, scalable, and AI-ready CoA for the future.

SMART – Core Optimization for CoA

🔹S – Standardization & Simplification

✅Reduce redundant GL accounts and align CoA structures across entities.

✅Use AI-driven tools to analyze transaction patterns and eliminate unused accounts.

✅Before Optimization: Multiple redundant GL accounts (e.g., 10001 - Advertising USA, 10002 - Advertising UK).

✅After Optimization: One standard GL (1000 - Advertising Expense) with dimensions for country-wise tracking. 📌 Use Case: A global FMCG firm reduced its GL accounts by 40% using AI-based CoA mapping, accelerating financial consolidation.

Journal Entry Example:

🔻Before Optimization

(Legacy CoA with Redundant Accounts):

Dr. 601100 - Print Marketing Expense $10,000

Cr. 200100 - Accounts Payable $10,000

🔹After Optimization

(Standardized CoA with Dimensions):

Dr. 601000 - Marketing Expense (Print) $10,000

Cr. 200100 - Accounts Payable $10,000

🔹M – Modernization for Digital Finance

✅Leverage S/4HANA’s Universal Journal for real-time reporting and insights.

✅Use AI for automated anomaly detection in financial reporting.

✅Before Optimization: Delayed insights due to batch processing of financial data.

✅After Optimization: AI-driven financial reporting with real-time cost tracking.

🔻Before Optimization (Manual Revenue Recognition):

Dr. 120000 - Accounts Receivable $50,000

Cr. 400001 - Sales Revenue USA $50,000

🔹After Optimization (AI-Powered Real-Time CoA with Dimensions):

Dr. 120000 - Accounts Receivable $50,000

Cr. 400000 - Sales Revenue (Region: USA, Product: Electronics) $50,000

📌Use Case: A manufacturing company used AI to flag unusual cost variances, reducing financial errors by 25%.

🔹A – Alignment with Compliance & Regulatory Needs

✅Map CoA to IFRS, GAAP, and tax regulations, ensuring global compliance.

✅Automate regulatory reporting using AI-based compliance monitoring.

✅Before Optimization: Different CoA structures across regions, leading to compliance challenges.

✅After Optimization: AI-powered real-time audit trails and automated regulatory reporting.

Journal Entry Example:

🔻Before Optimization (Manually Adjusted Depreciation Accounting):

Dr. 650010 - Depreciation Expense (Old CoA) $5,000

Cr. 170000 - Accumulated Depreciation $5,000

🔹After Optimization (IFRS-Compliant AI-Driven CoA):

Dr. 650000 - Depreciation Expense (IFRS) $5,000

Cr. 170000 - Accumulated Depreciation $5,000

📌Use Case: A multinational bank reduced compliance efforts by 50% through AI-driven reporting automation.

🔹R – Real-time Insights & Reporting

✅Structure CoA to enable predictive analytics and AI-powered financial forecasting.

✅Use embedded SAP analytics for real-time tracking of business performance.

✅Before Optimization: Fragmented revenue recognition across different CoA structures.

✅After Optimization: AI-powered CoA with real-time revenue forecasting.

Journal Entry Example:

🔻Before Optimization (Manually Categorized Cost of Goods Sold - COGS):

Dr. 500001 - Raw Material Cost $30,000

Dr. 500002 - Labor Cost $20,000

Cr. 210000 - Accounts Payable $50,000

🔹After Optimization (AI-Enhanced COGS Categorization in Universal Journal):

Dr. 500000 - COGS (Material: Steel, Region: APAC) $50,000

Cr. 210000 - Accounts Payable $50,000

📌Use Case: A retail firm leveraged SKU-level revenue tracking to optimize pricing and inventory planning.

🔹T – Tailored for Future Scalability

✅Ensure flexibility for M&A, new business models, and rapid expansions.

✅Use AI-based simulations to test post-merger financial structures.

✅Before Optimization: Rigid CoA structure unable to accommodate new business models.

✅After Optimization: AI-assisted CoA simulations for mergers, acquisitions, and expansions.

Journal Entry Example:

🔻Before Optimization (Different CoA Structures Across Merged Companies):

Dr. 702010 - M&A Costs - Company A $100,000

Dr. 800010 - Acquisition Costs - Company B $100,000

Cr. 100000 - Bank $200,000

🔹After Optimization (Unified CoA for M&A Readiness):

Dr. 700000 - M&A and Acquisition Costs $200,000

Cr. 100000 - Bank $200,000

📌Use Case: A healthcare company reduced M&A integration time by 40% using AI-assisted CoA restructuring.

SRI – Strategic Resilience & Integration with AI

🔹S – System-Wide Integration

✅Harmonize CoA across finance, logistics, and controlling for seamless operations.

✅Use AI-powered transaction matching to reconcile procurement costs.

📌Use Case: A pharma company reduced financial mismatches by 30% using AI-led reconciliation.

🔹R – Risk & Compliance Management

✅Implement AI-based fraud detection for risk-based account classifications

. 📌 Use Case: A telecom firm reduced financial fraud risks by 20% with AI-driven fraud detection models.

🔹I – Intelligent Automation Readiness

✅Enable AI-based auto-reconciliation and predictive financial analysis.

📌Use Case: A tech company reduced manual effort by 70% using AI-powered reconciliation automation.

Three-Step Advisory for CoA Optimization

Assess Your Current CoA Structure - Conduct a CoA health check—identify redundancies, unused accounts, and compliance gaps. Use AI-based tools to analyze historical transactions for pattern-based recommendations.

Design & Align Your Future-Ready CoA - Define the target CoA structure based on Global Best principles. Align with IFRS, GAAP, and tax regulations to ensure compliance. Implement AI-powered GL account mapping and auto-reconciliation features.

Implement & Monitor with AI & Automation - Migrate your optimized CoA to SAP S/4HANA Cloud). Set up real-time financial dashboards for tracking business performance. Enable AI-driven risk analytics and automation for fraud detection and compliance monitoring.

 

Key Takeaways

✔️Optimizing CoA is the foundation for a successful cloud-based finance transformation—without it, your digital initiatives may struggle with inefficiencies.

✔️The framework ensures that CoA is standardized, scalable, and AI-ready.

✔️AI and automation are critical enablers in streamlining financial operations, improving compliance, and accelerating insights.

✔️A structured three-step approach—assess, design, and implement—can make CoA optimization a smooth transition rather than a complex overhaul.

By making CoA optimization your first step, you set the stage for a truly intelligent, agile, and future-ready finance function.

 

Are you ready to take that step? 

Srinath Ganesan

entry.title: GRC, Trade and Tax with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I'm thrilled to share an update regarding our latest release - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 for GRC, Trade, and Tax. This update highlights many significant innovations from SAP Risk and Assurance Management, SAP Watch List Screening, SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, as well as SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance.

For a quick overview of the top release highlights for GRC with 2502, you can watch my video. If you're interested in a complete overview of all release highlights, scroll down to the table of contents.

 

 

This blog covers the following innovations:

SAP Risk and Assurance Management

  • Qualitative Risk Management Capabilities
  • Comprehensive Customer-Specific Reporting
  • Enabling Manual Procedures: Offline Execution

SAP Watch List Screening

  • Custom Watch List - Uploading of Custom Watch List Content
  • Unified SAP Fiori Apps and Process Optimization

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

  • Receiving Electronic Invoices From Suppliers By E-Mail (Germany)
  • Migration of Online Submission to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance

  • Extended Access Governance: Support for Additional Authorization in SAP BTP
  • Automatic Deprovisioning for Expired Business Role Assignments
  • Additional Risk Details in the Approver View Through a More Extensive Risk Perspective

 

SAP Risk and Assurance Management

Qualitative Risk Management Capabilities

The GRC Risk service offers a risk management solution with capabilities including risk documentation, risk analysis, and tracking of risk treatments. In addition to the existing quantitative risk analysis, it is now also possible to perform a qualitative risk analysis.

Value Proposition

  • Simple and quick - Enable users to assess risks without using precise numbers to determine impact or probability
  • Easy to perform and doesn‘t require expertise or training
  • Encourages collaboration and input from various stakeholders
  • Can be adapted to a wide range of industries and scenarios

Capabilities

  • Addition of qualitative risk assessment to the quantitative method
  • Qualitative risk assessment is a non-numerical approach to evaluating risks
  • Identify and prioritize risks based on their likelihood of occurring and the severity of their impact, using descriptive categories like low, medium, or high
  • Unified scoring to represent severity of risks regardless of quantitative or qualitative assessment approach to ensure comparability of assessment outcomes

 

Video 2: In addition to the existing quantitative risk analysis, it is now also possible to perform a qualitative risk analysis in SAP Risk and Assurance Management

For more information, see: Risk Analysis

Back to top

 

Comprehensive Customer-Specific Reporting

SAP Risk and Assurance Management supports comprehensive customer-specific reporting by making consolidated data available for reporting tools such as SAP Analytics Cloud.

This enables customers to analyze and visualize aspects like Number of Issues by Control over a Certain Time Period. 

Value Proposition

  • Analyze data related to different business objects to get deeper insights.
  • Optimized for reporting purposes.
  • Easy to use and simple to configure.

Capabilities

  • Consistent and consolidated data.
  • Provided via OData interface.
  • Consumable by third-party analytical solutions.

Fig. 1: SAP Risk and Assurance Management supports comprehensive customer-specific reporting

For more information, see: GRC Data Consolidation Service

Back to top

 

Enabling Manual Procedures: Offline Execution

Manual procedures can now be performed offline by procedure owners, meaning that they can be completed wherever they are needed. A template is available for download in the Offline Assigned Manual Procedures app which can then be distributed by compliance managers via email to all relevant people.

Value Proposition

  • Complete manual procedures anywhere, with or without an internet connection.
  • Aggregate data collected by several procedure owners in one place.
  • Leverage familiar tools like spreadsheets for greater ease of use.
  • Share templates with occasional business users in supported languages with minimal administration.

Capabilities 

  • Templates support fast and easy to use manual procedures to ensure that no time is lost.
  • Distribution of templates by compliance managers enables procedure owners to focus on the task at hand with minimal administrative effort.
  • Ability to perform manual procedures offline ensures maximum flexibility.
  • Enables compliance managers to have a better overview of collected data and ongoing tasks.

Fig. 2: In SAP Risk and Assurance Management, manual procedures can now be performed offline by procedure owners

For more information, see: Perform Offline-Assigned Manual Procedures

Back to top

 

SAP Watch List Screening

Custom Watch List - Uploading of Custom Watch List Content

If you wish to use a custom watch list for screening, instead of or additionally to the watch list provided by SAP, you can now use the Watch List API.
To select the watch lists that should be used for screening, you can use the new app Configure Watch List Screening.

Value Proposition

  • Use a custom watch list for screening

Capabilities

  • Replace the provided watch list or use the custom watch list as supplement

Video 3: With SAP Watch List Screening, it is now possible to use custom watch lists in addition to or as a replacement

For more information, see the Administration Guide:  Watch List API (optional)

Back to top

 

Unified SAP Fiori Apps and Process Optimization

You can now use a new app for ad hoc screening, a new app for reviewing screening hits and a new app for managing automatic screening decisions. The new apps have been designed to enhance your experience with more robust features, a streamlined interface and context-sensitive help. The new apps will eventually replace the old apps.

Value Proposition

  • More robust features
  • Streamlined interface

Capabilities 

  • Context-sensitive help

Fig. 3: SAP Watch List Screening now offers three new apps: one for ad hoc screening, one for reviewing screening hits and one for managing automatic screening decisions.

For more information, see: 

  • Ad Hoc Screening
  • Review Screening Hits
  • Manage Automatic Screening Decisions

Back to top

 

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

Receiving Electronic Invoices From Suppliers By E-Mail (Germany)

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance offers a process to validate electronic documents sent to you by your suppliers by e-mail to ensure they meet the European standard EN16931. You can use this process to comply with the new German regulation to be technically capable of receiving electronic invoices as of January 1, 2025.

When you receive electronic invoices or credit notes from your supplier by e-mail, you can upload the extracted electronic invoices (XML file) and create electronic documents for them in the Manage Electronic Documents app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. You can then validate them and display them in HTML format using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.

In addition, you can use SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management to automatically extract and upload the XML from the received email. The validation in the cloud edition can be automated as well as the posting of the supplier invoice for further processing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Value Proposition

  • Ensure compliance with the new German regulation to be able to receive invoices from suppliers electronically.
  • Ensure compliance with the German electronic invoicing standards:
  • XRechnung standard using the Cross Industry Invoice (UN/CEFACT) syntax.
  • XRechnung standard using the UBL Invoice or UBL Credit Note syntax.

Capabilities

  • Upload supplier invoices extracted from emails you receive from your supplier to the Manage Electronic Documents app
  • Creation of electronic documents for the supplier invoices in the Manage Electronic Documents app
  • Validation of electronic documents using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition
  • Display of supplier invoices in HTML format
  • Integration with incoming automation solution such as SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management

Fig. 4: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance allows you to validate electronic documents sent to you by your suppliers by e-mail to ensure they meet the European standard EN16931

For more information, see: Electronic Supplier Invoices (Receipt by E-Mail)

Back to top

 

Migration of Online Submission to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

In some countries/regions, you can submit your statutory reports electronically by using the web services provided by the respective government agencies. The end-to-end process of submitting statutory reports electronically to the authorities requires that you integrate your system with the service that actually transmits the statutory report electronically. This direct submission feature was supported via SAP Document and Reporting Compliance service and is now migrated to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.

Value Proposition

  • One single channel for all submissions across countries and processes
  • Simplify IT landscape and reduce total cost of ownership
  • Manage your integrations to tax authorities within your global SAP BTP account

Capabilities 

  • Centralized hub for all submissions to authorities, including reports and other requirements (e.g. HCM)
  • Standardized integration between SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition introduced with scope item 78L. One connection across countries and scenarios

Fig. 5: The direct electronic submission feature for statutory reports has been migrated to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

For more information, see: Submitting Statutory Reports Using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition

Back to top

 

SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance

Extended Access Governance: Support for Additional Authorization in SAP BTP

The SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance application enables comprehensive risk assessment of permission-level authorization data by extracting BTP roles and scopes, while also providing the capability to define rules.

Value Proposition

  • Empowers organizations to enhance security and compliance by enabling precise risk assessments at the permission level
  • Improved operational efficiency, reduced risk exposure, and strengthened trust in enterprise access management processes

Capabilities 

  • Define rules at BTP roles as action and scopes as permissions
  • Execute risk assessment at granular permission-level authorization data

Fig. 5: SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance enables comprehensive risk assessment of permission-level authorization data by extracting BTP roles and scopes

For more information, see: SAP Busines Technology Platform - Cloud Foundry | SAP Help Portal

Back to top

 

Automatic Deprovisioning for Expired Business Role Assignments

Implementing an efficient process to automatically deprovision expired roles eliminates the need for manual review and identification of expired assignments. This automation ensures that relevant audit information is consistently updated to accurately reflect changes in role assignments, supporting compliance and streamlined access management.

Value Proposition

  • Streamline the Deprovisioning Process: Utilize the "Deprovision Expired Business Role Assignments" job to automate the identification and removal of expired business role assignments, eliminating the need for manual review.
  • Enhance Access Control: Ensure that users are promptly removed from roles they no longer need or are unauthorized to access, helping maintain strict access compliance.
  • Maintain Accurate Audit Trails: Automatically update audit logs and the "Business Role User Assigned" tab to reflect the deprovisioning of roles, ensuring a clear record of changes for compliance and review purposes.

Capabilities 

  • Administrators can now automate the removal of expired business role assignments by scheduling the "Deprovision Expired Business Role Assignments" job through the Job Scheduler app.

Fig. 7: SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance enables automatic deprovisioning for expired business role assignments

For more information, see: Help Document: Deprovision Expired Business Role Assignments

Back to top

 

Additional Risk Details in the Approver View Through a More Extensive Risk Perspective

An improved inbox view for approvers in the Access Request app, offering a more extensive risk perspective with additional details on risk factors, functions, actions, and authorization objects related to access.

Value Proposition

  • Equip approvers with comprehensive insights into risks, actions, and permissions that contribute to elevated access risks for users.

Capabilities 

  • More-extensive risk information, including additional information concerning risk factors, functions, actions, and authorization objects associated with access for the Access Approver view

Fig. 8: In SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance, the inbox view for approvers in the Access Request app has been improved

For more information, see: Help Document: Access Request - Detailed Risk View

Back to top

 

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts. 

Follow us on @SAP and #S4HANA, and myself on LinkedIn and X.

 

 

More innovation can be found in the What's new viewer

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 

 

The Future of Business, Today: Get Your Access to AI-Assisted Features Thanks to this Limited Promotion Now! 

The 2502 update is a strategic investment that combines advanced AI with powerful enterprise management tools. Whether scaling your business, enhancing productivity, or building sustainable operations, its capabilities help support a more agile and resilient future.

  • Facilitate easier understanding and interpretation of financial data with insights from cost center reviews and explanations of fixed-asset depreciation keys.

entry.title: South Africa VAT Rate Change
entry.content:
The objective of this blog post is to explain, the impact of South Africa VAT rate changes in SAP Business ByDesign. This short guide will help you to maintain new VAT tax rates in SAP Business ByDesign.

On March 12, 2025, South Africa's Finance Minister Enoch Godongwana announced significant changes to the country's value-added tax (VAT) rate during the national budget speech. These changes are set to impact businesses and consumers alike.

VAT Rate Increase

Starting from May 1, 2025, the VAT rate will increase by 0.5%, bringing it to 15.5%. This will be followed by another 0.5% increase on April 1, 2026, raising the rate to 16%.

Maintaining New VAT Tax Rates in SAP Business ByDesign
To ensure compliance with the new VAT rates, users of SAP Business ByDesign need to implement the following settings:
1. Navigate to Business Configuration > Overview > Search for the activity Tax on Goods and Services

Click on Tax Rate Schedule > Add Row
Update VAT rate for the year 2025 valid from Date 01 May 2025 as shown below

Again, click on > Add Row to update VAT rate for the year 2026 valid from Date 01 April 2026 and then Click on Save to reflect the updated VAT rates.

By following these steps, SAP ByDesign users of South Africa can smoothly transition to the new VAT rates and ensure compliance with South Africa's updated tax regulations.

Thanks & Regards

Anusha

SAP Business ByDesign

entry.title: Week-Based (5-4-4) Calendar Year in S/4 HANA New Asset Accounting
entry.content:
Overview: 

In this blog, I aim to provide a comprehensive overview of the week-based calendar year (5-4-4) in SAP. This includes a detailed explanation of its business relevance, configuration steps and end user activities to help you to understand and implement effectively. 

Introduction:

In the world of financial & operational planning, business often follow various fiscal calendar structures to suit their reporting needs. One of the most commonly used fiscal structure is the week-based calendar year and the week-based calendar is classified as below.

  1. 4-4-5 ( Four weeks, Four weeks, Five weeks)
  2. 4-5-4 ( Four weeks, Five weeks, Four weeks)
  3. 5-4-4 (Five weeks, Four weeks, Four weeks)

The 5-4-4 calendar year is commonly used in industries like Retail, Manufacturing and Consumer goods. The traditional Gregorian calendar groups months unevenly, the week-based calendar or 5-4-4 calendar standardizes fiscal periods into consistent pattern.

The 5-4-4 calendar provides a more consistent comparison of weekly sales and operational performance across periods, since each period includes a similar number of business days and weekends. This model helps to avoid the complications of monthly calendar anomalies like varying number of days and differing number of weekends.

In SAP, Most of the standard reporting, financial periods & closing processes are designed around calendar or Non-calendar structures. Introducing a week-based model like 5-4-4 require rethinking of how you define the fiscal year variant, Mapping of posting periods and align with dependent modules. This change require or can have implications on both configurational changes and business process alignment. 

When a week-based calendar year is activated in accounting, Revenue Recognition aligns with the defined fiscal period duration, using a specified fiscal period-end instead of the traditional Gregorian calendar month-end. This process is particularly tailored for the Daily Ratable plan, under which Revenue Recognition calculates the daily revenue of contract and distributes it proportionally across the days within each fiscal period.

Since we're implementing the 5-4-4 calendar year structure for one of our clients, I wanted to share some key insights from the experience. During my research on this structure, I couldn't find much documentation(s) or discussions. To bridge that gap I am sharing my learnings and approach in this blog.

Business Use case: 

If a manufacturing entity runs its production operations, material planning and cost tracking based on weekly schedules. However the organization currently follows a standard month based fiscal year in SAP, which causes several operational disconnects: 

  1. Production cost don't align financial periods making it difficult to analyze performance
  2. Labor and overhead variances are uneven across months with different week counts.
  3. Weekly KPI's are not reconciled properly 

Solution:

By Implementing the week-based calendar year structure in SAP, we were able to help the customer effectively address the challenge outlined above. 

Post Implementation benefits:

  1. Cost efficiency in sync with actual weekly activities.
  2. Period end closings and reporting become consistent and comparable
  3. The business gains better control over cost trends, weekly variances and plant performance

Implementation Approach:

The week-based accounting calendar can be implemented in SAP using one of the two approaches depending on business requirement

  1. Beginning of the year 
  2. Mid of the year

Mid year: When switching to week-based calendar in the middle of your fiscal year, first configure a shortened fiscal year (to close out the old calendar early)

If you've already posted transactions in the current fiscal year or need to adjust historical data, you need to perform the System Landscape Optimization(SLO) to re-map and migrate existing postings.

As a best practice, it is recommended to implement the week-based calendar year at the start of the fiscal year 

Below are the configurational steps required

  1. Create a new Fiscal Year variant - OB29
  2. Set the Fiscal year to Short-end Fiscal year (Mid year)
  3. Maintain the periods - OB29
  4. Assign the Fiscal year to Company code - OB37
  5. Adjust the posting periods - OB52 (if require)
  6. Create Weightings - OA85
  7. Activate period weightings at depreciation level - OAYL

 Configuration walkthrough:

  • Create Fiscal Year Variant: OB29

SPRO - Financial Accounting - Financial Accounting & Global settings - Fiscal Year - Maintain Fiscal year variant 


 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  • Create weightings in Asset Accounting : OA85

SPRO - Financial Accounting - Asset Accounting - General Valuation - Fiscal Year Specification - Define weightings of periods - Weight periods in fiscal year version 

  • Activate period weightings at depreciation level : OAYL

SPRO - Financial Accounting -  Asset Accounting - General Valuation - Fiscal Year specification - Define Weighting periods - Specify area for individual period weighting  

End user Activities or Testing scenarios:

  1. Create Asset Master Record - AS01
  2. Post Asset Acquisition - F-90 
  3. Run Depreciation (AFAB) or Check the Asset values (AW01N)

Asset explorer: 

The values have been calculated according to the 5-4-4 proportion. For Period 1, the value is 1006.43 for period 2 it is 805.14, for period 3 it s 805.14. These same values apply for periods 4 through 12 as well.

Example:- 

Asset Acquisition = 5000/- 

Useful life = 7 Years

Depreciation value  5000/7  714.2851

To get a new proportion value for period 1 = 714.2857*0.09615  = 68.78

To get a new proportion value for period 2 = 714.2857 *0.07692  = 54.945

To get a new proportion value for period 3 = 714.2857 *0.07692  = 54.945

Same will repeat for the respective periods. 

Weighting Factor  = Weeks in period52 or 53 in case of leap year

For Instance, Weighting Factor  = 5/52 = 0.09615 

Model of 5-4-4 structure:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Note: It is advisable to coordinate with the CO,MM & SD Module teams to ensure the alignment and avoid potential integration issues during or after the fiscal year structure changes. Also, Make sure to check the period end activities & Custom report (if applicable)

Conclusion:

The 5-4-4 calendar brings structure and consistency to financial periods, aligning better with the weekly business operations. with the right setup and co-ordination, it lays the foundation for more streamlined reporting and smarter decision making.

entry.title: Your Guide to the Financial Management Track – Sapphire Orlando 2025!
entry.content:
Finance leaders, mark your calendars! SAP Sapphire & ASUG Annual Conference Orlando (May 19 – 21, 2025) is set to be a pivotal event for those aiming to revolutionize their financial operations. In today's rapidly evolving business landscape, finance functions are at the forefront of driving strategic decisions. SAP Sapphire 2025 provides a unique platform to explore how integrating AI, data analytics, and cloud technologies can transform financial operations, ensuring resilience and competitiveness.

Top Financial Management Sessions to Attend at SAP Sapphire & ASUG Annual Conference 2025 in Orlando

Whether you're managing uncertainty, navigating transformation, or enabling compliance and sustainability, this year's Financial Management Track will equip you with the knowledge, tools, and inspiration to lead change. Visit this page to explore the sessions, build your personalized agenda, and discover content tailored to your role.

🌟Spotlight Sessions

Gain powerful insights into driving business and finance transformation in these key sessions:

  • Finance Transformation with SAP Business Suite (Onsite: FIM3233) (Recording: FIM3233v)
    Learn how integrated applications, real-time data, and AI unlock new opportunities to optimize working capital, improve planning accuracy, and ensure compliance—automatically at DHL Information Services.
  • Transformation finance and spend through applications, data & AI (Onsite: FIM3235) (LIVE and Recording – FIM3235v)

🛣️ Roadmap and Solution Overviews

Gain foresight into SAP's vision for finance:
